items.q: Why does food provenance matter to consumers?
items.correct: It affects environmental impact, quality, ethics, and supports local economies.
items.random_wrong: It tells you the calorie content.
items.why: Students reduce food choice to price. Provenance (origin) raises questions of food miles, fair trade, animal welfare, seasonal availability, and supporting local producers - all consumer concerns.

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: Which group is biggest on the Eatwell Guide?
items.why: Correct answer: Fruit and veg. “Sweets” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Eaten occasionally. “Half your plate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many glasses of water per day is recommended (minimum)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
